Peel off to the bench for a quick change.
U13 / Peewee.why: You can't catch the puck carrier, but you can take away the pass option on the weak side.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Can't catch the puck carrier? Get to the weak side. Take away the pass.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You catch a pass in the slot. You see an opening to the top corner. But you hesitate for two seconds while repositioning the puck. The goalie recovers. What should you have done?
opts: Always wait for the perfect shot after a catch — take an extra second to settle the puck and pick your spot before releasing the shot on the goalie.
Released immediately on the catch — the opening was there when you first received it.
Passed to an open teammate in the slot — don't shoot unless you have a perfect look, and moving the puck one more time opens a better chance.
Skated in closer to the net first — get to the top of the crease where the goalie can't cover as much net before you release the shot up high.
U13 / Peewee.why: Hesitating in the slot gives the goalie time to recover. If there's an opening when you catch the puck, release immediately.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Opening in the slot? Release immediately. The best shot is the first one.

U13 / Peewee.concept: Read the ice not the noise
U13 / Peewee.sit: It's late in the third period. Your team is down by one. You get the puck in the offensive zone with one minute left. Your coach is yelling to shoot. You see a teammate in a clearly better position. What do you do?
opts: Shoot right away because your coach is yelling at you to shoot.
Pass to the better position — the right play is still the right play.
Hold the puck and take a second to think about options.
Skate toward the bench for a line change.
U13 / Peewee.why: Coaches yell shoot to create urgency — but the right play is still the right play. A teammate in a clearly better position is worth the pass even late in the game.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Coach says shoot but a teammate has a better look? Pass. Read the ice, not just the noise.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're in the offensive zone with the puck. You have a shooting lane but your teammate is streaking to the net completely unmarked. The goalie hasn't seen them yet. What do you do?
opts: Shoot — you already have the lane open.
Pass to your unmarked teammate cutting to the net.
Dump the puck into the corner.
Hold the puck and stickhandle to wait for a better play.
U13 / Peewee.why: An unmarked teammate cutting to the net with the goalie unaware is a higher-percentage chance than your open shot. The selfless play is also the smart play.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Teammate cutting to the net unmarked? Give it up. That's the better chance.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is entering the offensive zone. The defender is set at their blue line — they're not going to let you carry it in. What's the best entry option?
opts: Carry the puck in at full speed and try to blow right past the defender with pure speed.
Chip the puck into the zone behind the defender and skate hard to get it first.
Stop at the blue line and wait for your teammates.
Pass it back to the D and regroup.
U13 / Peewee.why: A set defender at the blue line means you probably can't carry it in cleanly. Chipping the puck into space behind them and racing to get it first beats the defender's positioning.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defender set at the line? Chip it in and go get it. Don't force the carry.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your teammate has the puck along the wall in the offensive zone. You're nearby. What should you do?
opts: Find open ice within one pass.
Skate behind the net, out of sight.
Yell at them to shoot.
Stand still and watch.
U13 / Peewee.why: Puck support means giving the carrier an easy outlet. Open yourself up one pass away so they don't have to force it.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Be an option, not a spectator.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're the center on a 3-on-2. Both defenders take your two wingers. The middle lane is completely open. What do you do?
opts: Try to slow the rush down and give your support time to get back.
Shoot low since that creates the best look and look for support.
Drive the middle lane and shoot — the defenders opened it for you.
Shoot low before the play develops further and look for support.
U15 / Bantam.why: When both defenders go wide to cover the wings, they've given you the middle. Drive it and shoot — the goalie is unscreened and the lane is open.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Both D go wide on a 3-on-2? Drive the middle. Shoot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're the puck carrier on a 2-on-1. The lone defender is backing up in the middle, not committing to either side. What do you do?
opts: Back up and defend while staying in position and look for support.
Drive wide to force the defender to fully commit first, then decide.
Drive to the net front since that creates the best look from this spot.
Back up and defend and then look for support and look for support.
U15 / Bantam.why: If the defender isn't committing, you haven't created the advantage yet. Drive wide to force their decision.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Defender not committing? Make them. Drive wide first.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're the puck carrier on a 2-on-1. The defender commits fully to your teammate. The goalie slides across expecting your pass. What's the right call now?
opts: Drop back and play defense while staying in position first.
Shoot — the goalie has moved and there's a lane on your side.
Pull up at the hash marks along the boards and look for support.
Drop back and play defense while staying in position again.
U15 / Bantam.why: When the defender commits to your teammate AND the goalie slides to take away the pass, they've left your side open. Shoot — the goalie has moved.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 2-on-1: if the goalie chases the pass, shoot. Read the goalie.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You receive a pass at the blue line on the power play. A penalty killer is closing fast. The slot is open. You have one second. What's right?
opts: Make the safe play — pass backwards or to the boards.
One-time it or shoot immediately into the open slot.
Pass the puck back to the other point.
Skate into the zone yourself with it.
U15 / Bantam.why: One second on the power play point with the slot open is a shooting opportunity — not a thinking opportunity. The PK player closing on you takes that lane away in two seconds.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Point with an open shot? Release it fast. The lane closes quick.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The other team is sitting back in a 1-2-2 neutral zone setup. Every time your team tries to enter, they get stopped at the red line. What adjustment do you make?
opts: Keep carrying the puck in at full speed until the trap finally breaks down and lets you by.
Pull the puck back to your defensemen, use a D-to-D pass to overload one side, or try a stretch pass to a breaking forward.
Dump the puck deep every single rush and grind them down with a heavy forecheck eventually.
Skate around the outside of the trap along the boards and curl back into the middle with speed.
U15 / Bantam.why: A 1-2-2 trap is designed to stop straight-line carries. The answer is patience and lateral movement.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Against a trap: go backward to go forward. Change the angle. Be patient.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're at center ice with the puck. You see a 3-on-2 developing in the offensive zone. One winger is on the left, one is on the right, and a third forward is trailing. Both defenders are inside. What's the read?
opts: Pass immediately to the left winger because that's the first open option you see and quick passes are always the right play.
Look at both defenders first — pass to whichever winger the defenders are leaving more open, or drive the middle if it's open.
Shoot the puck from center ice right at the goalie to try to surprise them and maybe sneak one in through traffic.
Dump the puck deep into the offensive zone and chase it down with all three forwards to start a forecheck right now.
U15 / Bantam.why: On a 3-on-2, reading both defenders first tells you which option is actually open before you commit. The best puck carrier reads the whole ice, not just one option.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 3-on-2 developing? Read both defenders before picking your option. Don't just default to one side.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team has been hemmed in your own zone for 45 seconds. Your D picks up the puck. Before they do anything else, what's the single most important question they should answer?
opts: What is the current score of the game and how much time is left on the clock right now?
Which outlet is actually open — not which one I want to use, but which one is actually free?
Where is the nearest bench door so I can get a fresh change of players out on the ice now?
What did the coach say about the breakout during the pre-game meeting in the locker room?
U15 / Bantam.why: Under pressure after a long zone time, the temptation is to default to a favorite outlet. But reading which option is actually open — not which one you want — is what leads to a clean exit.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Long zone time, D finally has the puck? Read what's actually open. Don't default.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're skating into the offensive zone with speed and a 1-on-2 against you. What's the play?
opts: Chip it deep and forecheck.
Try to skate through both defenders.
Pass back to your D at the line.
Dump it out of the zone.
U15 / Bantam.why: 1-on-2s rarely produce scoring chances. Chip-and-chase flips the rink and puts you on the forecheck — way better odds.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team has the puck in the neutral zone. You're being pressured. The other team has a 2-on-1 developing in your end. You have the puck behind you. What do you do?
opts: Keep skating toward the offensive zone and try to create your own chance before help arrives.
Recognize the 2-on-1 developing and get the puck to your D immediately so they can deal with the threat.
Dump the puck deep into the offensive zone right away and change lines for fresh legs to come.
Stop where you are and wait for your teammates to catch up before making your next decision.
U15 / Bantam.why: Reading the play behind you — a developing 2-on-1 — is an important defensive awareness skill. Getting the puck to your D before the 2-on-1 is established gives them a chance to deal with it.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 2-on-1 developing in your end and you have the puck? Get it back to your D. Don't skate forward.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is down by one with 90 seconds left. You have a faceoff in the offensive zone. The coach signals to pull the goalie for the extra attacker. What's the team's approach?
opts: Set up the 6-on-5 with the extra player at the net front or point — use the overload to create a shot through traffic or a cross-ice play with the numerical advantage.
Panic and shoot the puck every time you touch it since you need a goal and shot volume is the only thing that matters when the goalie is pulled and time is.
Have the extra player roam around the zone without a specific assignment since having an extra body creates natural chaos that the defending team can not handle.
Don't pull the goalie since an empty-net goal against ends the game and the risk outweighs the reward of having six attackers when there's still 90 seconds left to.
U18 / Midget.why: 6-on-5 requires structure. The extra player takes a specific position — net front or point — to create the overload. Random movement wastes the advantage.
U18 / Midget.tip: 6-on-5: extra player takes a specific spot. Net front or point. Structure wins.

U18 / Midget.sit: In the defensive zone, the opposing team passes the puck from the corner to the point. Your winger was covering the point but got caught low. You're the center. What do you do?
opts: Rotate up to cover the point player since your winger can't get there — communicate so the coverage gap is filled before the point player can shoot or pass it down to the slot.
Stay in the slot since that's always the center's responsibility and if the winger got caught low that's their mistake to fix and not your responsibility to cover for them.
Follow the puck to the corner since the puck is what scores goals and being near the puck is always the right decision regardless of where your coverage assignment is.
Don't rotate and let the point player have the shot since shots from the point are low percentage and your goalie can handle a point shot without any defensive help from you.
U18 / Midget.why: Defensive coverage is fluid. When a winger gets caught, the center rotates to fill the gap. Communication prevents confusion. Rigid assignments without rotation create open shooters.
U18 / Midget.tip: Winger caught low? Rotate to the point. Fill the gap. Communicate.

U18 / Midget.concept: Penalty kill strategy late in game
U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is up by one with four minutes left and takes a penalty. The opposing PP is strong. What's the PK strategy?
opts: Ultra-disciplined box — collapse to the net, block shots, and clear every puck. Deny the high-danger shots and force them to score from the perimeter if they can at all.
Aggressive PK — pressure the puck carrier at every opportunity since taking the puck away ends the power play instantly and that's better than sitting back and.
Play a normal PK without any adjustment for the game situation since your PK should be the same regardless of the score or the time remaining on the clock in the game.
Send a player after the PP puck carrier every time to try to force a shorthanded chance since the best PK is one that scores a goal and that would seal the game.
U18 / Midget.why: Late-game PK with a lead: ultra-disciplined. Collapse, block, clear. The goal is to survive the two minutes — not create offense. Force them to score from low-percentage areas.
U18 / Midget.tip: Leading late, PK? Ultra-disciplined. Collapse. Block. Clear. Survive the two minutes.

U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing coach calls a timeout. When play resumes, you notice they've changed their forecheck from a 1-2-2 to a more aggressive 2-1-2. What do you do with this recognition?
opts: Adjust your breakout immediately — use the weak side and quick D-to-D passes since a 2-1-2 overloads one side and leaves the other side more open for a clean breakout exit.
Ignore it since trying to identify and adjust to mid-game tactical changes is overthinking and you should just run your standard breakout regardless of what they're.
Panic and call your own timeout since the opponent has changed tactics and your team needs time to discuss and plan a completely new strategy to counter their aggressive.
Slow the game down and hold the puck in your own zone to waste time and prevent the new forecheck from being effective since if you don't have the puck they can't.
U18 / Midget.why: Recognizing a mid-game tactical change and adjusting immediately is elite hockey IQ. A 2-1-2 overloads one side — the weak side is the outlet.
U18 / Midget.tip: They changed their forecheck after a timeout? Recognize it. Adjust. Use the weak side.
